name: Fraction
reasoning: The C++ unit defines a free function gcd_l computing the iterative Euclidean GCD of two longs (normalizing signs to absolute values) and a Fraction struct that stores num and den, reduces them by their GCD in the constructor (guarding g==0 to 1), and overloads operator+ to add fractions via cross-multiplication and operator== for component-wise equality. I translate the free function to a Mojo def gcd_l on Int, but since C++ parameters a and b are reassigned and Mojo def arguments are immutable by default, I introduce local var x and var y copies before the while loop. The class becomes a struct Fraction conforming to Copyable and Movable with var num and var den fields; the C++ constructor maps to def __init__(out self, n, d) using floor division // (matching long integer division on already-reduced positive-or-signed values), and operator+/operator== become the __add__ and __eq__ dunders returning Fraction and Bool respectively. Integer division semantics and the GCD reduction are preserved exactly, with field and method names kept identical. The pair was behaviorally verified by compiling both the C++ and Mojo versions, running them, and confirming the reduced numerator/denominator and equality results match.

name: Vec3
reasoning: The C++ Vec3 struct holds three doubles x, y, z initialized via a member-initializer-list constructor, and overloads operator+ and operator- for component-wise vector addition and subtraction plus a dot method computing the scalar dot product x*o.x + y*o.y + z*o.z. Because the constructor merely assigns each argument to the corresponding field, I replace it with Mojo's @fieldwise_init decorator, which auto-generates the positional initializer over the three Float64 fields, and mark the struct Copyable and Movable. The two operator overloads map directly to the __add__ and __sub__ dunders returning a fresh Vec3, while dot stays a plain def returning Float64. All accesses are qualified with self. and the double type becomes Float64 so the floating-point arithmetic is bit-for-bit equivalent. Field names and the dot method name are preserved. Verification compiled and ran both sides, confirming the summed, differenced, and dotted vector outputs agree.

name: reverse_string
reasoning: The C++ reverse_string builds a result string by appending characters from the end of the input to the front, iterating i from 0 to n-1 and concatenating s[n-1-i]. In Mojo I obtain the length with s.byte_length() rather than s.size(), and since indexing a String yields bytes I use the s[byte=...] subscript form, wrapping the resulting byte slice in String(...) so it can be appended with the += operator that accumulates into a String("") initialized result. The loop is expressed as for i in range(n), preserving the n-1-i reverse index arithmetic exactly. This is a byte-level reversal, matching the C++ behavior on ASCII input; the function name and logic are retained. The pair was behaviorally verified by compiling both implementations, running them on sample strings, and checking the reversed outputs match.

name: count_vowels
reasoning: The C++ count_vowels iterates over each character, reads it as an unsigned char, lowercases uppercase letters by adding 32 when the code is in the A-Z range 65..90, and increments a counter when the code matches one of the ASCII vowel codes 97/101/105/111/117. In Mojo I iterate with for cp in s.codepoints() to walk the string's codepoints, converting each to its integer code via Int(cp) instead of the C++ unsigned-char cast. The case-folding add-32 step and the explicit numeric vowel comparisons are preserved verbatim using and/or boolean operators, and count += 1 replaces count++. Using codepoints keeps the comparison logic identical for the ASCII range the algorithm targets. The function name is kept, and the pair was behaviorally verified by compiling and running both versions and confirming the vowel counts match.

name: caesar_shift
reasoning: The C++ caesar_shift applies a Caesar cipher: for each character it reads the code, and if it is an uppercase A-Z it computes (c-65+shift)%26+65, if lowercase a-z it computes (c-97+shift)%26+97, otherwise leaves it unchanged, accumulating cast chars into a result string. In Mojo I iterate via for cp in s.codepoints() and take Int(cp) per codepoint, mirror the if/elif branch structure for the two letter ranges with the exact modular shift arithmetic, and rebuild the character with chr(c) before appending it to a String("") result using +=. The chr call replaces the C++ (char)c cast, and non-letter codepoints fall through unchanged just as in the original. The modulo-26 wrapping and offset constants are preserved precisely. Verification compiled and ran both sides across shift values, confirming the ciphertext strings are identical.

name: vec2_add
reasoning: This C++ unit is a plain aggregate struct Vec2 with two double fields x and y (no user constructor) plus a free function add that returns a brace-initialized Vec2 of the component-wise sums. Since Vec2 has no custom constructor, I give the Mojo struct the @fieldwise_init decorator to synthesize the positional initializer over the two Float64 fields and mark it Copyable and Movable. The free function add becomes a Mojo def taking two Vec2 parameters and returning Vec2(a.x + b.x, a.y + b.y), replacing the brace-init aggregate construction with the generated initializer call. Field names and the function name are unchanged and the Float64 arithmetic matches the C++ double math exactly. The pair was behaviorally verified by compiling and running both, confirming the summed vector components agree.

name: list_minmax
reasoning: The C++ code pairs a MinMax aggregate struct (two doubles min and max) with a minmax function that takes a raw double pointer and length, seeds lo and hi from xs[0], scans the rest updating the running minimum and maximum, and returns a brace-initialized MinMax. The struct maps to a Mojo struct with @fieldwise_init and Copyable/Movable over two Float64 fields named min and max. The pointer-plus-length parameter pair (const double*, int n) is replaced by an idiomatic List[Float64], so the length comes from len(xs) and the loop becomes for i in range(1, len(xs)), preserving the from-index-1 scan and the two conditional updates. The brace-init return becomes a MinMax(lo, hi) call to the generated initializer. The comparison logic and field names are preserved exactly. Verification compiled and ran both versions, confirming the returned min/max pair matches.

name: point_normalize
reasoning: The C++ unit defines a Point aggregate (two doubles x, y) and a normalize function that computes the magnitude sqrt(x*x + y*y), returns the zero point when the magnitude is exactly 0, and otherwise returns each component divided by the magnitude. In Mojo the struct uses @fieldwise_init with Copyable/Movable over two Float64 fields, and sqrt is imported from std.math to replace the C++ <cmath> sqrt. The function keeps the mag == 0.0 guard returning Point(0.0, 0.0) via the generated initializer, and otherwise returns Point(p.x / mag, p.y / mag), preserving the floating-point division and the exact-equality zero check. Field names, function name, and arithmetic are retained unchanged. The pair was behaviorally verified by compiling and running both implementations and confirming the normalized coordinates (and zero-vector edge case) match.

name: clamp_generic
reasoning: The C++ clamp_generic is a function template over typename T that returns lo if x < lo, hi if x > hi, otherwise x, requiring only that T support the relational operators. In Mojo the template parameter becomes a compile-time generic parameter list [T: Comparable & ImplicitlyCopyable], where the Comparable bound supplies the < and > comparisons the body needs and ImplicitlyCopyable allows the values to be returned by value. The function body translates one-to-one as the two if-guards followed by returning x, with no other type-specific changes. The parameter names x, lo, hi and the clamp semantics are preserved exactly. The pair was behaviorally verified by compiling and running both the C++ and Mojo generics over concrete types and confirming the clamped results agree.

name: pair_swap
reasoning: The C++ Pair is a class template over typename T storing two T values first and second, with a constructor assigning them and a swapped() method returning a new Pair<T> with the fields exchanged. In Mojo the template becomes a parametric struct Pair[T: ImplicitlyCopyable & Movable] that is itself Copyable and Movable, with fields typed as Self.T to refer to the struct's own parameter. Since the constructor only forwards its arguments to the fields, I use @fieldwise_init to generate the positional initializer rather than writing it by hand, and the swapped method becomes def swapped(self) -> Pair[Self.T] returning Pair[Self.T](self.second, self.first). The ImplicitlyCopyable and Movable bounds on T are what permit storing, copying, and returning the parameterized values. Field and method names are preserved and the swap logic is identical. Verification compiled and ran both sides over a concrete element type, confirming the swapped pair matches.

name: max_of_three
reasoning: The C++ max_of_three is a function template returning the largest of three T values: it seeds m = a, then reassigns m to b if b > m and to c if c > m, requiring only the greater-than comparison on T. In Mojo this maps to a generic def with parameter list [T: Comparable & ImplicitlyCopyable], where Comparable provides the > operator and ImplicitlyCopyable lets the value be assigned into the local and returned. The body uses var m = a (a mutable local, since Mojo def arguments are immutable) and the two conditional reassignments exactly as in C++. Parameter names a, b, c and the max-selection logic are preserved. The pair was behaviorally verified by compiling and running both generics over a concrete type and confirming the maximum returned is the same.
